sections.raw_yaml:
objection_inversion:
    description: |
      When the customer raises a cost-, time-, or interest-based objection,
      the high-leverage move is often to FLIP the objection — use their framing
      to redirect, not address the objection on its terms. The flip doesn't
      have to be logically airtight; it's a pattern interrupt that pulls the
      customer off-script and opens a crack for the real conversation.
    mechanism: |
      Breaks customer's autopilot. The "what?" moment is the crack the rep enters.
    triggers_and_flips:
      - {trigger: "I can't afford it", flip: "What you can't afford is your current bill"}
      - {trigger: "It's too expensive", flip: "What's expensive is NOT switching"}
      - {trigger: "I'm not interested", flip: "What you're not interested in is staying with what you have"}
      - {trigger: "$X is high", flip: "Quote their number back as the actual expense — solar replaces the expense"}
    when_to_use: "Any cost/time/interest objection in the first 5 minutes"
    detection_finding: |
      ZERO instances of this technique observed across 335-call corpus. This is
      Stewart's #1 daily coaching contribution — surface flip opportunities on
      every call where the rep made the safe move instead.

    actual_relationship: |
      Tesla is a LEAD SOURCE for Ion. Tesla forwards solar-interested
      customers to Ion as a referral partner. On a Tesla-sourced lead,
      the setter's job is identical to any other inbound: qualify the
      lead and schedule the closer. Product selection happens at the
      closer / proposal stage, not at setter stage.

      What Ion CAN sell into a Tesla lead:
        - Ion-branded solar panels (Ion's own product line)
        - Tesla Powerwall battery (only battery brand Ion carries)
        - In-house roofing (standalone or solar-prep)

      What Ion CANNOT sell:
        - Tesla solar panels (Ion sells its own panels, not Tesla's)
        - Tesla solar roof tiles (integrated shingles — not Ion's product)

      partner, Ion carries its own panels plus the Tesla Powerwall battery
      option, but Tesla roof tiles aren't part of Ion's catalog.
      SESSION10 (Jake/Larry) shows the failure mode when the rep doesn't
      reframe at all — but the gap is narrower than a "bait-and-switch."

numeric_discipline: |
    You may quote numbers spoken by the customer or rep in the call.
    You may quote numbers in this schema's context section.
    You may NOT invent, estimate, range, or imply savings, percentages,
    timeframes, or any quantitative claim not grounded above.
    Prescriptive alternatives use INTENT and EMOTION as leverage,
    NOT numbers. The pain the number represents is the leverage.
    The number itself has no feelings.
    When a flip or alternative would be sharper with a number that isn't
    grounded, FLAG it: "this leverage point would land harder if the rep
    knew the actual range — coaching gap on financials." Don't fill the
    gap with fiction.

strategy_recognition: |
    When evaluating rep responses, look for STRATEGY MATCH, not VERBATIM MATCH.
    Reps may execute a schema-defined strategy (curiosity_creation,
    bill_swap_reframe, spouse_inclusion, objection_inversion, etc.) using
    their own phrasing. Mark strategy-aligned variants as schema-compliant.
    Only mark as deviation when the strategy itself is wrong, not when the
    words differ.

proposed_categories.name: setter_scope_creep
proposed_categories.description:
Rep volunteers closer-tier content (product features, financial mechanisms,
warranty/cost rationales, panel specs) before the customer asks, or in response
to a question that should be redirected to the specialist.
proposed_categories.distinction:
protocol_violation breaks a procedural rule. setter_scope_creep gives away
appointment value by being accurate-but-premature. Content correctness isn't
the issue; sequencing is.
proposed_categories.coaching_drill:
Identify the moment of drift (the first technical question the rep should
have redirected). Replay the clip. Have the rep deliver the escape hatch
twice. Watch for the pattern across that rep's other calls.

proposed_categories.name: intro_legitimacy_omission
proposed_categories.description:
Rep opens the call without establishing company identity, lead source, OR
credibility anchor in the first 30 seconds. Includes cases where the company
name is mentioned but no context (state, referral source, certified-installer
framing) follows.
proposed_categories.distinction:
protocol_violation could be many things. intro_legitimacy_omission is
specifically the failure of the intro's primary job — answering
"is this a scam?" before the customer can ask.
proposed_categories.fix:
Two-sentence anchor: company + state + lead source. Adds under 10 seconds,
materially changes the call's trust baseline.

proposed_categories.name: objection_inversion_miss
proposed_categories.description:
Customer states a cost / skepticism / prior-experience objection that maps
to the codex's objection_inversion trigger. Rep accepts the objection at
face value, validates it, OR pivots to feature explanation instead of
deploying the bill-as-villain reframe.
proposed_categories.distinction:
empathy_miss is emotional. objection_inversion_miss is strategic — missing
the structural reframe move where the customer's stated reason against
solar is the same financial situation that justifies solar.
proposed_categories.coaching_drill:
The inversion concept — "the financial argument against solar is the same
financial argument FOR it" — needs to be drilled until reflex. The
objection_inversion_miss moments in the corpus are the clearest teaching
examples available.
proposed_categories.critical_finding:
Stewart's corpus analysis found ZERO instances of the objection_inversion
move being executed across the full 332-call curated corpus. This is the
codex's highest-leverage daily coaching contribution and it is completely
absent from the floor. This is a floor-wide training gap, not a per-rep
coaching note.

proposed_categories.name: prior_contact_probe_miss
proposed_categories.description:
Customer discloses a prior solar conversation, quote, design, or promise
from another company OR a previous Ion touch. Rep acknowledges but does
NOT probe for competitive intelligence (which company, what was shown,
what happened, customer reaction).
proposed_categories.distinction:
knowledge_gap is missing PRODUCT knowledge. prior_contact_probe_miss is
missing the competitive-context EXTRACTION that would sharpen the closer's
opening frame.
proposed_categories.fix:
Drill as verify.prior_designs reflex: hear "I talked to someone" → ask
one probe question before moving on. "Did they show you a design?
What did you think?"

proposed_categories.name: unanchored_soft_exit
proposed_categories.description:
Call ends without a locked appointment time, a verbal "is that fair?"
handshake, OR a confirmed next-step OWNED BY the rep. Includes passive
closes like "look forward to hearing from you" instead of active closes
like "I'll send you a text confirming Thursday at 2."
proposed_categories.distinction:
conditional_booking has a real unresolved customer barrier. unanchored_soft_exit
is procedural fragility — missing the handshake mechanics that convert
passive agreement into active yes.

proposed_categories.name: non_standard_scenario_hold
proposed_categories.description:
Rep places customer on hold mid-call to consult a supervisor because the
customer's situation falls outside the standard new-solar-install script:
off-grid, battery-only, manufactured / mobile home, removal / reinstall,
cash-buyer.
proposed_categories.distinction:
knowledge_gap is general product unknowns. non_standard_scenario_hold is
specifically the gap in non-standard scenario RECOGNITION and ROUTING.
proposed_categories.fix:
Build a non-standard scenario quick-reference for setters covering the
five most common edge cases (off-grid, battery-only, manufactured home,
removal/reinstall, cash buyer) with a defined routing action for each.
Goal: not eliminate escalation, but reduce holds that stem from missing
basic routing pattern.

proposed_categories.name: curiosity_creation_failure
proposed_categories.description:
Customer hands the rep an anchor (kWh number, bill amount, prior quote,
neighbor's system). Rep either files the data silently, delivers a generic
feature-adjacent comment, OR moves to the next verify item without using
the moment as a forward-pulling hook.
proposed_categories.distinction:
bill_anchor covers failure to COLLECT the bill. curiosity_creation_failure
catches when the data has already been disclosed — the failure is
converting disclosed data into appointment motivation.
proposed_categories.fix:
Drill the reflect-and-forward-pull habit. When a customer gives you a
number, say it back with a forward frame.
Example: "13,000 kWh a year — that's exactly the kind of usage where the
design gets interesting. Let's get you on the calendar."

proposed_categories.name: softener_overuse
proposed_categories.description:
Rep uses uncertainty-softeners ("kinda", "kind of", "I guess", "maybe",
"I think") repeatedly in declarative statements about Ion's program,
products, or process.
proposed_categories.coaching_drill:
Pull the rep's top-5 softener-density moments. Replay them. Have the
rep deliver the same line WITHOUT the softener. Compare audibly. The
difference in confidence and authority is immediate and obvious.
Reps fix this fast once they hear it.
proposed_categories.attribution:
Spencer-identified pattern from 13 years of sales operator experience,
sharpened by observing his son onboard at Ion. "Softeners make reps
sound unprofessional — they say it to soften direct statements, but
it just signals uncertainty. Drop them and the call's quality goes way
up. So does the rep's confidence."
